Since day one player, over 1300 hours, one of my top most played games, lots of time streaming and making memories with friends AND NOW...no more.

For the TLDR, game bad. Carless greedy company, the worst gambling of loot boxes I've ever seen, no concern for the quality of experience for the players, laughable attempts of quashing the constant surge of cheaters, severly limited servers with no option of choice, insane ratio of in game currency value to the dollar and on top of all that is an extreme lapse of quality of in game content.

Understandably, there are many different points of view and likes accross many different personalities so there is great varience in opinions and therefore not everything is for everyone. Aside from previous satement there is still some measure of quality that is objective and I'd like to dive into those over personal feelings or experience.

Most importantly is quality of Publisher/Developer. Naturally, the ideals and desires of the two are often differing, conflicting, independant, selfish. Why not, one will care more about face, money and status while the other concerns themselves with quality of product and it's users. Uncertain as to which one is more reasponsible for the copious negatives BUT there is almost nothing possitive to say about anything purtaining to the responsibilities of quality in game that either company oversee. Gambling is quite possibly one of the worst things to be implemented in casual and competitive gaming in order to create a strive for goals and the rewards of their completion and PUBG is without a doubt the worst one in the gaming industry containing: Several different tabs to visit to obtain in game cosmetics, a couple different unique currency PER TAB, obscure ways to obtain said currency, painfully slow gain of currency, the value of the items woeully insignificant to the total value of game time and possibility to earn, multiple loot box systems with a miniscule chance to earn anything valuable, grotesquely unfair ratio of covertion rate of real dollar to premium currency with next to no way to gain outside of spending real money.

There remains a fair deal of cosmetic content to gain without dropping a single dollar and it REMAINS a choice for the user if they wish, which I sadly have chosen and lament the thought of calculating that total value. Possibly sitting somewhere in the highest of tripple diggits. As it stands, not an ounce of this game is pay to win, all of the currencies prelude to just cosmetics and PUBG has some of the flashiest character designs, gun vehicle and even lobby skins BUT unlocking them is akin to an enternity of scraping your nails on a chalkboard and hoping that the next minute might yield gold.

Another point is the game mechanics. As a drop in, loot, survive and winner winner chicken dinner it is safe to say that, while not being the first in it's genre, PUBG has set the ultimate ground work and become synonymous with the game type, Battle Royal. Veritably the most saddest example of a "where are they now?" scenario like a slap in the face is that of PUBG now, similar to discovering your childhood hero is infact in rehab for various illegal activities. Which isn't to say the overabundance of cosmetics and crossover deals has interfered much at all with the overall game experience, just look at fortnite, everyone loves a bit of whimsy in their lives. Saddly the level of detail in gunplay, the strategey unique in many Mil Sims, the squad based gameplay and skill levels and outcomes have ALL been on a rapid decline. Quality of gameplay systematically making way for the glam and glitter designed to distract players into painfully forcing themselves into the meat grinder with the epitath of "just one more".

Futhermore understandable is the claim of "get good" in a game of competitive challenge. With most games, a progression from beginner to top player is meant to scale with the player, combining challenge with reward further cultivating an overall positive experience which provides opportunity to "get good". However many new player, returning player or just a casual drop in and out player are met with matchups agaisnt skill levels that belong only in the eSports level, denying the first five to ten steps required to obtain "good" status. Personally to say after 1300+ hours I have very little skill to show for time spent, of which at one point I commited time and effort to train myself. Learning in game physics, selecting guns to master an understanding of, developing of tactics and even perfection of the drop system, like the broken shattered dreams laid before me I am left with naught but ruin and frustration. After all that I'd like to point out, only ever casual gaming, never trying for anything other than drop in and play. That level of standard for a game is grotesque in it's entirety, solidifying the game primarily as a private club of people built only on the extremely skilled player. 

Throughout the many years PUBG has been alive, it has seen far more than it's fair share of cheaters. Myself and friends of mine have personally seen some of the worst cheating in our gaming experiences, from the simple terrain cliping or expliot, wall hacks, auto aim, zero recoil to some severe diobolical cheating. I personally witenessed a kill cam reveal a player from across the map, lock instantly onto various people positions throughout the map, move through ground, wall and building in seconds to end up right behind me. While the appeal of a free game is very attractive to many players, there is always a little caveat of the previously mentioned problems such as high loot box focus, agressive battle pass, small to moderate percentage of the game locked away ABOVE ALL lies the biggest achilies heel in the form of cheaters. There is ZERO recompense for these cheaters, bans easy to work back around, accounts to be made as infinatly as you can create emails. Understandably there can be little done sometimes, if someone wants to cheat and ruin other players games, they are going to BUT one simple way that can stop cheaters or at least a vast majority of them is a game price. You can't bounce back from a ban anywhere near as easily if it costs you financially.

Finally as a nail in the coffin, I have not once seen a game require in order to simply use a feature of it you phone number, personal phone number, information which is only likely needed to track or steal information. Security is an extremely garbage reason to require personal information in order to play ranked modes as opposed to quick match. Systems across the whole of game space in the entirety of ever have never once needed that type of feature and it should come as a precursor for what either the game devs or publishers really care about which is tracking it's users.

Whilst personaly I've had my own ups and downs, possitive and negative memories, pros and cons, loves and hates and despite this length of a review, I could fill this space double over with my own personal thoughts and experiences. This game and it's makers care and hope for only one thing, the money they can bleed out of it's users while they distract players with the flashy lights of pretty outfits and instill false hope that the random chances are remotely achieveable. Solid 0/10, this game deserves to be laid to rest.

我是一名普通的《绝地求生：战场》老玩家，当初真心喜欢这款游戏，为了账号、为了更好的游戏体验付出了时间与金钱，可账号被盗、无故被封之后，只剩下满心的心酸与无力。
 
我曾经无比热爱这款游戏：写实的枪战竞技手感、每一局紧张刺激的生存博弈、和队友组队拼到最后成功吃鸡的热血，都让我一次次沉浸其中。我认真打磨枪法、积累赛季奖励、用心收藏每一件皮肤与道具，一点点把账号养成了自己珍视的心血，也心甘情愿为游戏付费，只为获得更好的游玩体验，把这里当成了日常放松、结交好友的自留地。
 
可意外来得猝不及防，账号不幸被盗，等我费尽心力找回账号的那一刻，迎来的不是失而复得的喜悦，而是账号被永久封禁的冰冷通知。盗号的人恶意违规、使用外挂作弊，最后所有的恶果，却要我这个原号主、合法氪金、本分游戏的普通玩家来承担。
 
我一遍遍走申诉流程，一次次提交被盗证据，换来的永远是冰冷的模板回复，账号根本没有办法解封。自己真金白银花钱买下、用心经营许久的账号，一夜之间化为乌有。那种叫天天不应、叫地地不灵的委屈与心酸，实在难以言说。明明我没有任何违规行为，却要为他人的恶行买单；明明我是被盗的受害者，最后却成了被平台惩罚的人，所有的投入、所有的回忆、所有的热爱，全都被轻飘飘的封禁通知打碎。
 
如今回头再看，游戏本身的玩法依旧出色，但糟糕的账号安全机制、冷漠低效的被盗申诉通道，真的狠狠寒了老玩家的心。普通玩家维权无路、氪金账号被盗封后无处说理，付出的时间、金钱与热情，全都打了水漂。
 
如果问我还会不会推荐其他人入坑，我只能说：游戏本身的竞技乐趣值得肯定，但极差的账号被盗保障、不公平的连坐封禁处理，会让每一个认真游玩、真心付费的玩家随时面临一无所有的风险。如果你没有极强的账号防护能力，没有能和低效客服长期拉扯的精力，真心不建议轻易大额投入、深度扎根。只希望官方能多听听普通被盗玩家的心声，完善被盗核验机制，还给无辜受害者一个公道，别再让真心热爱游戏的人，输得一无所有。

PUBG sits at the top of the charts with the kind of consistency that demands acknowledgment even from people who have zero interest in playing it, and I am one of those people.

Krafton essentially invented the modern battle royale template with this one. Everything from Fortnite to Warzone to every other drop in and survive game that followed owes its existence to what PUBG established, and that legacy is real regardless of how you feel about the genre it spawned. Being the blueprint matters even if the blueprint isn't your thing.

The problem is that being first and being best are different conversations and PUBG has always sat more comfortably in the former than the latter. The graphics have improved from the genuinely rough early access state but never quite shed that utilitarian quality that made the original feel like a tech demo that accidentally became a phenomenon. For a game that charges what it charges and has the playerbase it has the presentation has always felt like it was running to catch up with its own popularity.

The genre itself is the real barrier for a lot of people. Dropping into a shrinking zone with a hundred strangers and spending the first ten minutes finding a weapon is a specific kind of tension that either hooks you completely or leaves you cold, and there is no in between. If it doesn't grab you in the first few hours it never will.

Battlefield scratches the large scale multiplayer itch with actual structure, defined roles, vehicles, and objectives that make every match feel purposeful rather than arbitrary. That's a completely defensible preference and not one that requires much of a fight to justify.

[h1]Overall Verdict: The Only Battle Royale That Actually Scratches the Itch[/h1]

I came to [i]PUBG[/i] looking for a break from the other "popular" battle royales, and honestly, none of them ever scratched the itch quite like this one. There is a specific kind of intensity here—especially in the final circles where everyone is locked in holding sniper angles—that is just unmatched.

[h2]My Experience[/h2]

At the start, I spent a lot of time just fighting the map itself. I’d get so caught up in the sheer scale of the world that I wouldn't pay attention to the time or the encroaching circle, leading to a lot of preventable deaths. But, once I got the hang of it, that same massive map became a total sniper’s heaven. 

The difficulty curve, however, is wild. I’ve had sessions where I’ve cleared 20+ kills, feeling completely invincible, and other days where I struggled to find even a single kill. The jump in difficulty between lobbies is insane, which makes for a pretty jarring experience sometimes.

[h2]The Good & The Bad[/h2]

[b]Pros:[/b]
[list]
[*] [b]Unrivaled Intensity:[/b] The final circles are genuinely heart-pounding.
[*] [b]Sniper Gameplay:[/b] The map design makes for the best long-range combat in the genre.
[*] [b]Satisfying Highs:[/b] When the game clicks, it is an incredible rush that other BRs just don't deliver.
[/list]

[b]Cons:[/b]
[list]
[*] [b]Matchmaking Inconsistency:[/b] The skill gap feels massive; some days I'm dominating, other days I'm getting stomped immediately.
[*] [b]Mode Fragmentation:[/b] While you can toggle into FPP while playing TPP, it’s frustrating that some modes are strictly locked to FPP only. I really wish there was more flexibility to choose your perspective across all modes.
[*] [b]Confusing UI:[/b] Personally, I really don't like the menu system. It feels confusing, messy, and way too busy.
[*] [b]Ranked Access:[/b] I've never even been able to queue for a ranked game; the mode just doesn't work for me at all.
[*] [b]Technical Concerns:[/b] I’ve definitely had moments where I had to report players for what felt like blatant cheating.
[/list]

[h2]Verdict[/h2]

If you want a BR that feels tactical and rewards patience over pure twitch reflexes, this is the one. It’s flawed, and the matchmaking can be a rollercoaster, but when it’s good, it remains the gold standard for the genre.

7 Years of Loyalty, 0 Days of Optimization
I’ve been playing this game since 2017, right from the day it launched. I’ve stuck with it through every meta and every map, but my patience is finally running out. It’s painful to watch a game with such unique gunplay being slowly killed by corporate greed and poor technical management.

The Reality Check:
Performance & Freezes: It is 2026, and we are still dealing with stuttering, random freezes, and general performance issues. I have a high-end build, yet the game feels like it’s struggling to breathe. Why is optimization always at the bottom of the priority list?

Skins over Substance: Instead of fixing the core mechanics or addressing the long-overdue move to a new Unreal Engine, the developers are obsessed with pumping out "sloppy" skins and ridiculous cosmetics that ruin the game's atmosphere. We wanted a tactical battle royale, not a digital fashion show.

The "Engine" Excuse: We keep hearing how "difficult" it is to transition to a modern engine. Krafton is a multi-billion dollar company. Smaller studios manage to upgrade their tech, but here we are, stuck on an outdated foundation because the focus is strictly on monetization.

Killing the Vibe: The game is losing its identity. Every update brings more "nonsense" content and less stability.

Verdict: If you’re a veteran from 2017, you know exactly what I’m talking about. We love the core of PUBG, but the management is running it into the ground.

Dear Developers: Stop painting dresses and start fixing the engine.


"I cannot recommend this game in its current state. As someone who has been here since 2017, it's heartbreaking to see the devs prioritize K-pop skins and expensive progressive crates over basic game stability. We are still waiting for the UE5 transition while the current client suffers from performance drops and freezes. Great gunplay, terrible management."

With 465.6 hours in PUBG: BATTLEGROUNDS, all played with friends, I’ve had more than enough time to experience what this game does best—and where it still struggles.
The realism is one of the strongest aspects. Gunplay feels grounded, and every fight has weight to it. Especially when it comes to sniping, no other game really matches the satisfaction PUBG delivers. Landing a long-range shot or clutching a fight with your squad just hits differently.That said, it’s hard to ignore how clunky the game can feel, even after all these years. Movement, animations, and general responsiveness sometimes feel outdated compared to newer titles, which can be frustrating in intense moments.Despite that, PUBG still has something special. Playing with friends creates unforgettable moments, whether you’re messing around or trying to win seriously. The tension, the pacing, and those high-stakes endgames keep you coming back.I would recommend it if you enjoy realistic battle royale games and especially if you have friends to play with. Just be prepared to deal with some clunkiness along the way.

PUBG: Battlegrounds — 9/10
Before Fortnite. Before Warzone. Before any of them. There was PUBG, and it changed everything.
Say what you want about the state of battle royale today — every single one of those games owes its existence to what PUBG built from the ground up. The 100 players dropping on an island, the shrinking circle, the looting, the tension of the final ring — PUBG invented the template that an entire genre just copy-pasted and dressed up differently. This is where it all started.
And honestly? It still plays better than most of its imitators. There's a weight and grittiness to PUBG that nobody has quite managed to replicate. The gunplay feels real and punishing. Bullets have travel time and drop. You can't just spray and pray — you have to think about range, positioning, cover, vehicle sounds in the distance, footsteps on the floor above you. Every engagement feels genuinely tense in a way that flashier battle royales rarely achieve.
Erangel is still one of the best maps ever designed for the genre. Open fields, dense towns, chokepoints, rivers — it forces every kind of combat and never feels unfair. The other maps each bring something different to the table and after all these years there's still a ridiculous amount of variety.
Yes it had a rough early life. Yes the optimization has been a journey. But as a pure, unfiltered battle royale experience built on real tactical foundations, nothing touches the original.
The genre grew up and moved on. But the peak? It was always here.
9/10 — The original. The blueprint. Still the best.

Imagine spending 20 minutes playing "Interior Designer" in a derelict Russian shed, meticulously collecting a Level 3 backpack and a suppressed M416, only to be erased from existence by a guy laying in a bush with a crossbow.

Why You'll Hate It:
The Physics: Vehicles behave like they were programmed by someone who has only ever heard physics described over a bad phone line. Hit a pebble? Your car is now a satellite.

The Combat: You don't "fight." You loiter in a bathroom for ten minutes until the Blue Zone—the game's personification of tax season—forces you out to get shot by a 12-year-old with the reflexes of a cobra on meth.

The Optimization: It runs like a brick through a blender. Your PC will scream in agony just to render a texture that looks like wet cardboard.

The Verdict:
It’s a toxic cycle of looting, running, and dying to things you can't see. It’s miserable, unfair, and technically broken.

PUBG offers a high-stakes, tactical battle royale experience where 100 players fight to be the last one standing. With realistic weapons, intense combat mechanics, and a shrinking battlefield, every match is packed with suspense. Players must loot for gear, strategize, and use the environment to their advantage, while staying ahead of the deadly blue zone. Team play adds an extra layer of strategy, making communication crucial for success.

The game’s graphics and sound design are immersive, and while it has occasional performance issues, it's optimized for a wide range of PCs. However, the community can be toxic, and newcomers may find the learning curve steep. Overall, PUBG remains one of the top battle royales, offering depth and excitement for fans of strategic shooters.

Pros: Tactical gameplay, great maps, intense action, regular updates.
Cons: Steep learning curve, occasional performance issues, toxic community.

My only real issue is that it’s the ONLY program/game I have Windows installed for on a dual boot. I’ve been on Linux for years, and it’s nice to see more people giving it a shot. Proton is getting better by the second. But having to jump through hoops just to play this individual game is a bit frustrating. I basically have a whole different OS installed just to play PUBG.

It kind of feels like when I almost purchased a PS5 just to play FF7 Rebirth when it came out (I borrowed one).

PUBG is not perfect. I've played from the E.A. days so I've seen how bad things have been as well as seen the positives.

Pros: Game has a grounded feel, not super-crazy movement hell for more slow paced gameplay. The hit detection has been massively improved and rarely feels off now. QoL has gotten much higher without killing how the game feels. Personally, I also like the near-instant TTK as long as you have the skills to pull it off. Guns feel mostly varied and solid to use, nothing in the game besides melee weps are nearly useless.

Cons: Some games, RNG screws you so you get garbage / ring spawns on the other side of the map and you travel 15 minutes to the other side just to be instantly killed reaching the ring after 0 action. I personally haven't noticed many cheaters but you'll usually run into one within a few hrs of playtime and others seem to complain of it a lot more. Half of the maps are reaaaally not good with far too much area to span with low vehicle numbers or no cover or just flat out play like butt.

Overall it is a game that has been fixed up from the early days and somewhat forgotten since. It's a game where I instantly understand when someone doesn't like it or feel a mutual rush when a friend hits a nasty shot. There's a lot of good and bad in PUBG but I'd say with some patience you can learn to vibe here.

PUBG was my first proper keyboard-and-mouse shooter back in 2018 when I built my first PC. It wasn’t just a game - it was my introduction to getting humbled by strangers from 400 metres away.

PUBG is a slow-burn paranoia simulator disguised as a battle royale.
It’s 20 minutes of looting, 5 minutes of repositioning, and 0.4 seconds of getting headshot by someone you never saw.

The gunplay? Heavy.
The recoil? Personal.
The footsteps? Louder than your heartbeat.

This isn’t slide-cancel chaos. It’s tactical, punishing, and sometimes unfair - which is exactly why winning feels insane.

I’m running top-of-the-line hardware, a clean Windows install, and fully updated drivers yet this game constantly suffers from micro stutters.

This is NOT a hardware issue and NOT a software configuration problem. The problem is the game itself. PUBG runs on an outdated engine that clearly struggles with modern high-end systems and high refresh rate monitors. Frame pacing is inconsistent, and the experience never feels truly smooth, even on the best possible setup.

The game used to run much better in the past, but after recent updates the performance and smoothness have noticeably degraded.

If you’re using modern high-end hardware, don’t expect a flawless experience. The engine needs serious optimization.

100% classic Battle royal, I've played since release or maybe it was open beta. This battle royal is the roots to many BR's, but it came after H1Z1 and this is how it plays: there are lean mechanics, you can choose games of third person or first, the movements are blocky, but pretty fair and  skill based. The player base is the only downside to the game as the match making isn't fun due to cheaters, robots, or people who've had a lot of hours consistently. There are many game modes and not every game will you face constantly max level players so for the times you don't face cheaters or insane players you have great games that are extremely complex with different ways the fights go down

Example: I've ran over people with vehicles, done drive-by in the vehicles of the game, been killed by people shooting out of the plane vehicles, had enemies and teammates crushed by the "Deployable cover flair" when it drops 2 large cement structures very fast, killed 10 players with crossbows (hard difficulty)(achievement), grenades  of many different varieties (including blue zone grenades - copied from fortnite, but good move), deaths from the many different special weapons that drop from the red crates, and so much more.

One map had a gulag, but i think it's out of rotation now, but they instead also took the concept of being revived by teammates just as other BR's did and that is a good  change.

The game mode of 88 bots and 12 players is the most enjoyable to get warm and ready to face harder opponents. I hope for any of you reading this that you get to play versus people your own level. Whatever you do, dont let your first game be with that lvl 100+  friend... ENJOY IT WHILE YOU CAN.

There is a team deathmatch game mode, it takes place in small circles of random spots on the actual maps so that's awesome for getting warmed up

The games are exhilarating, Balanced well to where all guns are useful, you can choose to play safe, slow, fast, like a rat, or  however you like. Ghillie suits are a thing and they're fun. 

I've only seen 3 bad changes and they were map changes, but the new items added, new vehicles, skins, gameplay (Except the Match Making), emotes, etc... is all pretty great.

This game has gone through some wild shifts and changes over the years, and it is a battle royale first and foremost above all others, so if that is not your vibe, steer clear. 

That said, for a free to play adaptation from a pay to play core, PUBG has thrived since it's initial ups and downs of finding a good foothold in a market that quickly got overtaken by flashier and more cartoony competitors.  

This is a great game to just casually knock out a few drops with some friends or goof off in solo que doing self imposed absurd challenges, and the casual mode doubly makes it much less of a chance of just getting completely stomped by people who eat sleep and breath the game while your just trying to play with people of varying skill level and interest in the game. 

As it stands now, it is a solid free to play BR option for the people who prefer less cartoony asthetics with more realistic style firearms. It certainly is not a full hardcore vibe on the realistic feel so won't really scratch the itch there, but overall it has a lot going for it in it's specific niche.  The competitive scene is also quite impressive and for such a random variable element of a game that often comes down to luck over skill in terms of equipment strategies, it is still very entertaining to watch in spite of the inherent lack of a certain degree of mechanical balance optimization just due to the nature of the game itself. 

There are also very amusing custom lobbies that can be tweaked into all kinds of improvised game modes for very silly group fun if your lucky enough to be part of a community that can muster the numbers to host such a thing! 

The journey of improvement and refinement is a constant one that never truly ends however, and these are the things I think could do with some changes to improve the quality of the game and extend it's reach even further: 

[b] CASUAL REWORKS [/b] 

Change casual ques to 90min instead of 60min and allow groups who are queing together to go off the time remaining of the group member with the most time remaining, even if other group members have 0 time remaining themselves. people meet up and decide to do a little casual play at different points in the day, in different circles, this helps make this process not feel like a time triage excercise similar to scheduling DnD groups 

[b] BOT DROP [/b]

Adding a purely co-op comp stomp mode would be very helpful for those of us who enjoy playing casuals but do not want the human competitive element in any way beyond friendly team competition to see who can get the most kills / coolest kills. 

Someone like me can go toe to toe easily in the main matches and the "who's a bot and who's not" excitement of casuals is something I can handle easily enough, but for a lot of people I play with who enjoy a purely casual against bots experience that is not high pressure so they can be comfortable and spend time with friends and loved ones without getting upset or too overly stressed, something like this would be much better: 

The maps are much smaller environments (they can just be zoomed in sections of already existing maps to start with, but eventually custom designed maps using pre-existing assets would be the best course of action to help with differentation at a glance and variety) 

The game mode would be 100 participants, with up to 6 six slots occupied by players in one team replacing a bot spawn from either a pre-que or public lobby (with players still able to launch on less than max numbers) 

Everyone parachutes in at the same time in random spots in the sky above the map and have to steer themselves into locations alongside all the bots, and then it's just a free for all as the bots do not have teams, but they always priortize engaging human players over one another (and are generally coded to miss each other as much as possible when fighting one another) they will never kill downed players intentionally, and as the match progresses and the circle tightens, the bots become universally stronger, more well equipped, and deadlier, all while ensuring their top priority is to avoid the closing circle, up to the point of not stopping to engage until they are inside of it at all costs (and also reducing outside of circle damage to the bots so a ton of them don't just die out there like in casuals) 

I could elaborate a great deal on more specifics and unique elements of this game mode, but this review only has so much room so if the devs would like to contact me directly, I would happily provide additional expansion and development plans to streamline it's inception!

[b] FAT TRIMMING [/b] 

 There is soooo much overlapping emergency key jingling from this games years of desperate reinvention and expansion that needs cleaning up. There are so many absurd sub menus and six different currencies doing the same things that could all be solved by one that make the monetized elements far more complex and annoying to engage with. 

Making a "give me all my stuff I qualify for" button that just lists a history of where and why a thing was given to the player is so much more effective and gives people a less micro management heavy way of cleanly aquiring things. It should also store and log those qualified rewards until that button IS clicked even if the event or whatever was going on has passed, so that people don't accidentally miss out on things they qualified for simply because of an arbitrary timer. 

In addition, make the actual BP shop it's own thing that makes it easier to navigate what is available purely with BP without mixing it with the premium currency shop. You can still put advertisements in the BP shop items directing too premium shop versions, and you can add a premium shop currency buy options for all the BP items too even, but make the designation more clear, and simple to navigate 

Again, I have a massive list of UI reconfigurations and changes to offer in great detail, along with many mechanic revisions and refinements, if the developers are interested. I understand the limitations of development especially on a skeleton crewed F2P investment anchor, but I know how to work on nothing budgets with little resources, so happy to offer my expertise if desired! 

[b] NO MORE SOLO DANCES [/b] 

This one is mildly irksome, but ALL dances of every kind should be group dances. This is a nitpick I will admit it, but it's just unnecissarily exclusionary in a largely team incentivizing game. Add a toggle when activating the dance for it to be solo only if you really want to keep that for more mean spirited players, but don't enforce exclusion. 

I could go on an on and on about ideas for new events, tweaks and optimizations, promotional tie ins and baseline improvements but, again, due to limited space here, I'll just end on saying this game is not perfect by far, and it will be infuriating at times both because of the PVP nature of the game, and some of the games baked in elements at it's core, but if you want a slower paced BR That has less frenetic energy than other BR's and Arena Shooters while still being an engaging experience with a bit more of a "real life" influence, this one is the game for you! And it's free, and there are random little events that pop up free game modes to play with so, it's worth keeping installed just to mess around with now and then!

[b]PUBG  is a battle royale shooter focused on realism, tactical gameplay, and high-tension survival matches.

✅ PROS

-Tactical and realistic gunplay – positioning and strategy matter
-High tension in late-game situations
-Large, varied maps with different playstyles
-Free-to-play with regular updates

❌ CONS

-Clunky movement and animations
-Performance and optimization issues
-Steep learning curve for new players
-Can feel frustrating for casual players

🧠 Final Impression

PUBG delivers a tense and strategic battle royale experience where every mistake can be fatal. While it shows its age technically, it still shines for players who prefer realism over arcade action.

👉 Recommended for players who enjoy tactical, high-stakes shooters.
👉 Less ideal if you prefer fast-paced or casual battle royales.[/b]

This is my third review of this game on this store page. The first time I reviewed the game with an extremely positive outlook and message about the game. Once I then edited it for the first time, I got a lot more vile and negative about the game. I want to come on here and do a proper review instead of basing it off of old fun I had on the video game or anger/bs I have dealt with on the game within the last x amount of hours I have put into it. I have played this game since it came out just about. I believe the first time I played this game was early 2016 if not late 2015. I have played over a few steam accounts, on my Xbox quite a lot, and even on my phone. I have also spent a lot of time watching some of my favorite streamers play it (mainly Shroud). I have a lot of love for this game. I love its visuals, I love the weapons, most of the gunplay is quite enjoyable, the movement is quite nice, etc. However, this game has a lot of issues that lie underneath its surface. These issues lie mainly within the community, but some are indeed within the game itself. The first main issue is the cheating, and yes I'm sure a lot of you will read that and think I'm just complaining, have no skill, and got destroyed one time and needed to come on here and mention it. That is not the case. Currently, I am 24 years old. I have spent a majority of my life gaming, on all types of games from survival games, roguelikes, city builders, shooters, so on and so forth. I have tried most TYPES of games that are on the market. I have spent time gaming on numerous platforms.. games have cheaters. Simple as that. It has been proven. Millions and millions of players have been banned from games for cheating, and a lot of the same cheaters make new accounts constantly to continue cheating, even if they have to pay. The only way this is usually stopped is by banning someone through a device ban, IP ban, or some more extensive bans that require a little more of a look into how the person is doing their cheating. It is a problem. In so many games. Unfortunately in some of my most loved games, including PUBG and Counter-Strike, the cheating is quite high up there. With high numbers of cheaters and low successful attempts to get rid of said cheaters. These people exist in high numbers. They show themselves doing so on social media platforms even. They do not care, they are relentless. I recently came onto the community hub of this game to do some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ about it. I will say, It was mainly out of anger, and I went a little far with what I had said. But I had a few gentlemen comment on said forum saying some things that got me thinking. They fought what I had said so hard, coming at me for the way I spoke, for my "lack of skill in the game" that they certainly know because they have TOTALLY watched me play the game, and even trying to attack my person in saying I am immature, my behavior is "inadequate" etc. In this lies another one of the main issues within the games community. The ignorance, and the toxicity. The same ignorance and toxicity that seems to lie within a majority of the population, over any subject that they believe they know every little thing on, while doing little to no research. It baffles me that the world has come to what it has. The people who sit on the internet all day long, commenting to people and/or making their own posts, with full ignorance while trying to call out someone else's ignorance. Everyone has a little ignorance, of course, no one knows absolutely everything. It is just absolutely appalling to me how some people could go into a subject, with absolutely no information or research to back their opinions, just solely based off of how they feel because they think they know it all, and absolutely attack others character, opinions, and ideals over it. Sit down, do a little bit of research, figure out that the world sucks and people destroy things simply for fun. It exists, it sucks, but it does. Don't attack people for the way they feel. And then ironically continue to call that person immature, and inadequate while you are actively showing immaturity and inadequacy in your behavior. Stop reflecting the way you feel about yourself onto someone else who you have never stepped foot near, never spoken a real word to, and never sat down and had a true conversation with to understand what they have been through and what they think. As far as the issues that lie within the game itself, they are simple. The protection against cheaters is inadequate, they have taken away from the games realism by adding items that destroy the games core aspects, they have changed certain parts of the game that just absolutely strip it of its potential. it is saddening. All of this is saddening. And this spans over all of the MMOG video games that are on the markets these days, it hurts my heart. At this point in my gaming life, I will probably soon reach a point where I cannot continue to play largely MP games as it makes me lose hope for the people in these communities on a daily.

[h1][b][i]Diary of a PUBG unc[/i][/b][/h1]
The first time I played [i]PUBG: Battlegrounds[/i] was back when it was still called [i]PlayerUnknown’s Battlegrounds[/i]—[b]you had to pay back then to be a toxic ass.[/b] Despite it being PvP, not my favorite hobby, it grabbed me by the balls, its grip firm yet gentle, and from September 2017 till July 2018 I spent around 250 hours looting, sneaking, killing, dying (mostly dying) and sometimes eating chicken.

I still remember queuing up for my first solo, riding the plane, being mesmerized by the giant map, all the funny sounding names, and one stood out—[b]school[/b]. I thought, [i]“School? Who would want to revisit childhood random boner trauma, as it greeted you in the middle of algebra class, you just sitting there, praying to whoever is listening up there, that the teacher doesn’t pick you to come up front and explain to everyone what the author really meant when he wrote: [b]“In men there are two wolves.”[/b]I’ll go there, it’ll be empty for sure!”[/i]

It was in fact, not empty

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3652187216
[h3][b][i]Hoarder Sim[/i][/b][/h3]
Now, the main reason I even stepped foot onto Erangel, all those years ago, was [b]the looting.[/b] It’s just so zen, yet it’s also one giant [i]risk-reward[/i] game: deciding what to carry with you, what to drop, what to swap out—[b]I get all tingly from just thinking about it.[/b]

But what loot do you loot?
[list]
[*][b]Weapons:[/b]
You need to kill people, and melee-ing is so passé. So, bullets are the answer—[b]headshots[/b] or [b]spray and pray[/b], dealer’s choice. As years passed, the game’s arsenal expanded, which for a loot monke like me, seeing so many guns all around me, it’s basically better than the sex! My current favorites are the [i]AUG[/i], [i]SCAR-L, VSS[/i] and [i]M249[/i]. The [i]Crossbow[/i] also gets a thumbs up—[b]very hard to kill people with, much dying in chaotic gunfights because it’s pretty useless in 99.999% of situations I found myself in.[/b] Looks cool tho! Oh, there’s also grenades. [b]Pro Tip:[/b] do try and kill the enemy if you are using frags, m’kay?

[*][b]Attachments:[/b]
Think of guns like puzzles with a piece or two missing—[b]that’s the attachments, the missing piece(s)![/b]. These little doohickeys are very important, like little [b]gun buffs[/b] if you will. They can improve [b]stability, recoil, magazine size, add scopes, etc.[/b] Hot take—[b]pimping out your gun with attachments is more fun than winning[/b]—(can’t) change my mind!
[/list]

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3652204471
[list]
[*][b]Consumables:[/b]
There’s many things to consume nowadays. Your favorite uncle consumes Fox News, you consume Hentai, and so on. In [i]PUBG[/i] you just kinda have first aid kits, bandages, etc. They heal the body and soul tho. But, there are exceptions—pills, [i](def not Monster)[/i] energy drinks, adrenaline shots, you can fill up on these and get a nice passive boost, pushing you past human limits. Why use first aid when you can heal by overdosing on pills and [i](not Monster)[/i] energy drinks? Plus, sounds cooler when the coroner reads your cause of death too.

[*][b]Gear:[/b]
Ok, so guns kill, attachments attach, and you consume consumables, what’s left? Well, I’m pretty naked right now, and don’t have deep pockets, so where all those [i](not Monster)[/i] energy drinks gonna go? What about ammo? Can my skull stop a Kar-98k bullet? Well, lucky for me, the map is littered with [b]helmets, vests, and backpacks[/b], all there to help my inner hoarder or protect me from other playerses nasty bulletses—[i]they want to steal my precious (loot)![/i] Gear come in three flavours, with Level 3 gear often found in hot zones or care packages dropped randomly during a match. Also, we now have a Jammer Pack. The standard [i]PUBG[/i] Jammer Pack does two things—[b]it’s a backpack and a jammer[/b]—stopping the nasty Blue Zone from hurting us… at least while its battery lasts that is.
[/list]

[h3][b][i]Two in the face, one in the ass’ base[/i][/b][/h3]
But you’re a man’s man’s man! You don’t play [i]PUBG[/i] to loot (I do tho), you play it to be the one who Chicken Dinners!

You have two options for dining, [b]Third Person[/b] and [b]First Person.[/b] Both allow [i]solo, duo and squads[/i]. And for those feeling extra masochistic, or just with big PP, you can drop [i]solo into duos/squads[/i] matches. I? Well, in the immortal words of Demi Lovato: [i]“I do it solo!”[/i]—[b]just me and my trusty right arm.[/b] But both duo and squads can be fun too!

Ok, so now we in: the plane is plane-ing and it's time to decide where to land. You’ll see many locations on the map, some with big names, some with smol names. [b]Big name = Big loot = Hot Zone.[/b] This is where I was taught the true essence of [i]PUBG[/i] combat—[b]don’t be a ♥♥♥♥♥![/b] I learned to loot faster, be more aggressive in gunfights, use my surroundings and audio cues to pinpoint players, and ofc, how to camp when enemies are all around me. In short, [b]hot zones[/b] are essential for your [i]PUBG[/i] growth, shaping men into slur-throwing boys—basically a [i]CoD[/i] lobby for the new generation: [i]“What?! How did he kill me? This ***** **** *** ***** ***** was clearly cheating! Report!”[/i] [b]—Average [i]PUBG[/i] enjoyer[/b]

Also, they have another [i]non-game[/i] related benefit that the penis doctor mafia doesn’t want you to know about—[b]landing in hot zones solved my ED.[/b] The surge of adrenaline, the blood pumping vigorously through [i]all[/i] the veins and arteries—[b]no magic pill can match it.[/b] So, next time, before laying down with [i]pillow-chan[/i], fire up a solo session and land in any hot zone, like [i]school[/i]—[b]you and the missus will thank me later.[/b]

And it’s when bullets start flying, and people start dying, [i]#rhyme[/i], that [i]PUBG[/i] turns into a fat lady and sings—it’s [i]perfection![/i] Even I, a loot monke, ended up loving the gunplay, despite sucking at it—[b]it’s just that damn addicting.[/b] Gunfights are a perfect showcase of your mastery of the game's mechanics and systems. Combat is not about who has the best or biggest gun—[i]brain[/i] beats [i]bullet[/i]! ... But [i]hack[/i] also sees [i]brain[/i] through the wall, 20km away, and can one-shot you, so…

In short, [i]PUBG[/i] gunfights are like getting ready for a date with that deSarmas girl from [i]Bladerunner[/i]. You pulled out all the stops, even bathed—[b]“Smell travels faster than light”[/b] —[i]Blindstein circa 2005 BC.[/i] You put on your best clothes, accentuating those love handles, so she knows you a good provider. And as soon as you step out, a Boeing 420 crash-lands on your head. It was an emergency landing, the pilot ate too much spicy Indian food and Boeing forgot to put toilets on their plane. You’re dead now and the toilet is clogged.

[h3][b][i]Chicken Winner[/i][/b][/h3]
But is [i]PUBG[/i] fun?

Well, let me tell you something you already know. [i]PUBG[/i] ain’t all sunshine and rainbows. It’s a very mean and nasty place, and I don’t care how skilled you are, it will send you back to the Lobby and keep you there permanently if you let it. You, me, or nobody is gonna hit as hard as [i]PUBG[/i]. But it ain’t about how many you kill. It’s about how many times you can die and keep playing. How many hacker headshots you can take and keep playing. That’s how [i]PUBG[/i]-ing is done!

In short, [i]PUBG[/i] fun.
I had fun.

PUBG – A game that works best when you bring the whole village
PUBG is a game I’ve played since release, and I’ve never played it the way the developers probably imagined. I’ve never been a solo guy, never a duo guy. PUBG is, in practice, a minimum three‑man game — and four is obviously best. Anything less feels like going to war with a water pistol and a smile. It’s simply not the same without a stable crew you know well.

The best thing about PUBG is the teamwork and the group dynamics. The laughter, the stupid mistakes, the idiotic situations, and the moments that are only funny when you’re playing with people you know. PUBG is basically a social simulator disguised as a battle royale. It’s not the game that makes it fun — it’s the people you play with.

Realism? No, we don’t do that here
Let’s be honest: PUBG is not realistic. Not even close. The vehicles behave like they’re made of rubber and low self‑esteem. Clothing has zero effect. The physics are… creative. But it doesn’t matter. The gunplay feels good, and it has improved over time. First‑person helps if you come from games like Battlefield, where the weapon feel is so good you almost want to send flowers to the developers.

Either way, PUBG is far better than CS, which gives me allergies, and miles better than Arma Reforger, which is absolutely terrible in that department.

The maps are great, and after many years with only one map, it’s actually refreshing to get some variety. Each map has its own challenges, and it keeps the game feeling fresh.

Tactics: Think ahead or die hungry
PUBG is tactically fantastic. If you don’t think ahead, you’re not getting any chicken dinner. The sound design is also excellent — with good headphones you hear enemies clearly, even though it’s usually just your teammate sneaking around and scaring the life out of you.

The pace used to be higher, with less loot and more desperation. I stopped playing for years, but came back because a friend wanted to try it again. I don’t regret it. The game is better now than it used to be, even if some people will swear the opposite. Opinions are like… well, you know.

Irritations: Almost gone
PUBG used to have growing pains, but now it mostly runs the way it should. I rarely bug, almost never crash, and the game feels stable. I think I’ve crashed once in six months.

I haven’t — as far as I know — encountered cheaters in a long time. It used to be worse, which is strange considering the game is now free. Free games usually attract unruly gamers from various countries who should probably spend more time on manners and education. It’s a bit like how certain countries’ behaviour in the world seems to rub off on their gamers online.

Balancing isn’t really a problem. Either you’re a noob, or you understand what’s going on. Eventually you get better than others, and then you play ranked.

Movement and controls: Still a bit stiff, but improved
PUBG has always had a slightly stiff feel, a bit like Arma, but it has improved over time. It probably comes from the Arma/DayZ mod heritage, but that’s just my theory. Either way, it’s much better now than before.

Teamwork: Alpha and omega (and minimum three players)
I always play with people I know. PUBG is, in practice, a minimum three‑man game, and four is obviously best. Playing with randoms only happens if we forget to turn off “fill squad,” and that usually reminds us why we don’t do that.

Communication is everything, spotting too. Pay attention to your colour, use it when you call things out, and stick with your group. That’s how you survive, and that’s how PUBG becomes the game it’s actually meant to be.

Conclusion
PUBG is a game that’s at its best when you play it with people you know. It’s tactical, fun, frustrating, social, and full of moments that only work when you have a regular crew to share them with.

1. When they added new maps, but no one wanted to play those maps, so they took away the ability to choose which map to play on. Man, I just want to play on Erengal. That's the only map I want. 

2. I switched to Linux. I went back and forth between Linux and Windows. And I never really got the feeling to put effort into being good again after I couldn't choose which map I wanted to play (reason 1). So, I just never bothered to switch over to Windows just to play one game. This game is not Linux compatible. I assume it's due to the anticheat. 

3. I did switch back to Windows on my main gaming PC for a couple of months this year and decided to give PUBG another shot, but I found out that I have to deactivate a windows 11 security setting (Kernel Protection) to EVEN FRICKIN' PLAY THE GAME. TLDR; To get me to play this game again, it would need to:
1. Allow choosing which map you play on
2. Gain Linux Support
3. Not use Kernel Level Anticheat or require me to make my system less secure just to play it.

I remember playing this for one or two matches when it came out; it was a fun and interesting atmosphere at the time (2017). It seems as if PubG, however, has not only created a long-lasting Battle Royale trend in First-Person Shooter gaming, it has pushed gaming more-so into gambling and non-emergent gameplay. 

Many newer gamers now think First-Person Shooter games are about waiting for people to come into your line of sight. This problem of waiting for your opponent to come to you goes beyond 'camping'. It actually speaks to how the common-sense military tactics (flanking, moving as a group, having an end goal, using the right equipment) you would see in First-Person Shooters has been diluted and forgotten. 

In addition, I find that modern players in First-Person Shooters (and in gaming at large) are more interested in spectating their own participation in Battle Royale games. It's like a self-reflexive voyeurism where they are so obsessed with the goal of 'winning' a match, or 'winning' a firefight that they seek to avoid any and all mistakes. It's like an athlete who is too scared to play an opponent for fear they might lose...and that is exactly how most firefights in PubG play out: one player, or group, waits for the other players to make a move. This isn't tension, it's merely a lack of strategic options within the game design.

Simply put, the Battle Royale genre is not designed for player interaction; more-so player participation. It's the difference between building something yourself and having someone else build things for you. 

If you've been around video-games for a decent amount of time, you can intuitively sense that Fortnite, Minecraft and Roblox are not 'building block games' but more-so digital service platforms where players use artistically-devoid assets to 'create their own fun'. There is a space for throwback pixel-drawn, simplistically colored games, so long as they are actually inspired by the past. These new 'creative' platforms (which draw in children in droves, more-so than gaming did in the past) are inspired directly by LEGO products. To end this tangent briefly: LEGO was once about building creative sculptures or toys that children could call their own, that is not the case any longer. The goal of LEGO is artificial scarcity and highly-inflated consumer prices. Most LEGO products are now incredibly expensive and usually rare. Static 'sets' that adults collect and never interact with have become the norm; the most incredible LEGO creations are the ones you go to see at museums...not the simple ones children made at home for themselves and their family members.

Collecting, negative interactions, scarcity and waiting...all these actions are elements that PubG emphasized in its First-Person Shooter design. You collect guns, you counter-intuitively avoid most combat engagements, the player count of the match is forcibly whittled down, and you are usually waiting for others to drop out or become vulnerable. 

This combination of gameplay elements forms the 'gambling' aspect mentioned earlier: how other players move around the map and whether these players interact with others (or your own self) is always somewhat random; random, that is, until the playerbase starts to realise that the win condition of PubG (and all Battle Royale games) is merely to stop playing all-together and observe how the emergent behaviour of each player shapes the match. 

Just like Lego sets, you collect...observe others and meticulously avoid 'interacting' with the match. 

This overall aesthetic of Battle Royale shooting may always be pleasing to anti-social types: you never really have to shoot your gun in player-to-player engagements, you can just wait for others to show their backs and sides to you. The Battle Royale aesthetic is also thrilling to play once or twice in the short-term when you have players on a 'level' playing field where they don't know the map, when there is a large enough playerbase that is still learning the game's mechanics, or when players actually choose to 'hunt' knowing full well they could be the hunted. 

The problem, however, is that the design of PubG -- and all subsequent Battle Royal games --is that most players will constantly take up statue-esque positions away from other players, engaging in too-scared-to-move behaviour patterns. The need for in-game gratification will also cause players to cheat, leave the player-base all-together or become intensely attached to the extremely-slim possibility that they will be the last player standing (usually from no active gameplay of their own). 

Nothing about Battle Royal games encourages the player to eliminate threats with their guns.

It is almost the inverse: you are encouraged to let lesser 'experienced' players eliminate the threats for you. 

There was some room for improvement in this genre when it was bubbling, but I think that time has passed. Earlier influences like Arma 2's Day Z mod, Escape from Tarkov and even the much-earlier released STALKER had various horror-like elements which created real tension and dread. The win condition in all three of those titles is survival at the player's own pace; both the environment and other players can ruin your session. The so-called 'PvE' and 'PvP' experiences were never seperated: they were one and the same.

...On a side note, this lack of separation between PvE and PvP is what really let the Survival game genre boom in the 2010s. All these trends were happening around the same time and its worth exploring why there's a relation between Battle Royale and Survival games...but this review is about PubG!...

The win condition in PubG is quite different from these survival-focused First-Person Shooters: PubG curates survival at the expense of other player's misfortune. The huge caveat of PubG is that the most successful player is the one who avoids real confrontation, observes the match and waits for their accolades. 

It is like hitting a jackpot on the slot machine.

PubG is a PvP game where the second 'P' is slowly effaced from what's supposed to be a multi-player match. This fundamental design aesthetic of the Battle Royale genre to 'remove other players' also removes the chaos, the hand-eye reflexes and general 'fun' atmosphere of First-Person Shooters. There is more to be said about the inclusion of 'operators' with 'special abilities' and pre-designed, cosmetic characters in Battle Royale games like Apex and CoD's Warzone...but this review is already long enough. 

I'd recommend playing PubG at least once, but there's no real substance to this game after one or two matches.

Let's be clear: PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds is not just a game. It's a 100-person therapy session where the couch is a military-grade jeep and the therapist is a stranger named xX_Shotgun_Sniper_Xx who just ended your 20-minute looting spree with a well-aimed pan.

I have a love-hate relationship with PUBG, and by "hate" I mean the kind of pure, unadulterated rage that makes me immediately queue up for another match. This game is a beautiful, janky, heart-pounding masterpiece of chaos.

The PUBG Experience, in Three Acts:

Act I: The Serene Looterella.
You drop onto the island. The sun is shining. Birds are... probably dead, let's be real. But for you, it's a peaceful shopping spree. You're in a secluded compound, humming to yourself as you pick up a nice backpack, a solid vest, and enough energy drinks to power a small festival. You feel like a post-apocalyptic Marie Kondo, organizing your inventory until everything sparks joy. "This sparkly helmet brings me joy. This extended quickdraw mag? Immense joy." This is the calm, the zen, the beautiful lie.

Act II: The Sudden, Deafening Symphony of "WHAT WAS THAT?!"
A sound. A single, distant crack. Your heart instantly becomes a drum solo. You are no longer a person; you are a pair of eyeballs glued to a screen, sweating pure adrenaline. You crawl through a field, believing yourself to be a shadow, a ghost, one with the grass. In reality, you are a very obvious man in a bright yellow shirt commando-crawling through a meadow. Someone from 500 meters away, using a scope you didn't even know existed, politely informs you of this fact. Your screen goes black. The panic was real. The failure is absolute.

Act III: The Unlikely Hero (or the Glorious Pan-ic).
But sometimes... sometimes you are the one who gets the drop. You stumble into a house, and a guy is there, looting a body. You both freeze. In a shared moment of pure, unscripted comedy, you both empty your entire magazines into the wall next to each other. In the ensuing panic, you switch to your trusty frying pan and, with a triumphant BONK, you emerge victorious. You didn't win the match, but you won that moment. And you will tell the story of "The Pan of Destiny" for weeks.

The Verdict:

PUBG is janky. The cars sometimes have a personal grudge against physics. You will get stuck on a tiny pebble. You will be killed by the dreaded "Blue Zone of I Forgot to Pay Attention."

But you know what? I wouldn't have it any other way. It's this beautiful, unpolished chaos that creates the most unforgettable gaming stories. It's the game where a cross-country road trip with three randoms in a UAZ turns into a lifelong (in-game) friendship, only for it all to end in a fiery explosion because Steve saw an airdrop and "just had to go for it."

It's tense, it's ridiculous, and that moment when you're in the final top 10, heart pounding in your ears, is a feeling few other games can replicate.

Final Score: 99/100. (Docked one point for that time I punched a fence for a solid minute instead of vaulting it.)

123 hours of my life spent hiding in bushes, crawling through fields like a confused moose, and being one-shot by someone I never saw. And honestly? I'd do it all again, eh.

PUBG is the granddaddy of battle royale - the original chicken dinner before everyone and their nan jumped on the genre. Sure, it's got more jank than a rusty pickup truck in Sudbury, but there's something about the gunplay that just hits different. When you actually win a firefight, it feels earned.

The maps are massive and the variety of strategies keeps things fresh. You can be a hot-drop hero or a loot goblin who spends 20 minutes finding the perfect loadout only to die to the first person you see. Both are valid life choices, apparently.

I've spent more time in the loading screen than I care to admit, and the optimization has been... let's say "inconsistent" over the years. But when the game runs smooth, it runs real smooth.

The squad experience is where this game truly shines. Nothing brings friends together quite like collectively screaming about vehicles, debating whether that's an enemy or a tree, and then getting wiped by a single grenade.

Winner winner chicken dinner, bud.

PUBG is still one of the rare battle royale games that nails the tension and thrill of survival. The gunplay feels weighty, the maps are smartly designed, and every round carries that pulse-quickening “anything can happen” energy. It’s the kind of game where positioning, strategy, and timing actually matter—you feel every shot, every peek around a corner, every clutch decision.

That said, cheating remains a persistent thorn. No matter how good you are, running into a hacker can instantly ruin the immersion. It’s frustrating, and it’s the one thing that keeps PUBG from being perfect. But when you’re playing legit, with a squad you trust, it still delivers one of the most satisfying and intense battle royale experiences out there.

PUBG was the first battle royale I ever got for my PC, and it immediately set the standard for the genre. Me and a buddy played it religiously for months, and the game delivered nonstop tension, excitement, and unforgettable moments. Every drop, every fight, and every final circle felt unique and rewarding.

PUBG nailed the balance between realism and chaos, making each match feel meaningful and intense. This game will always stand out as one of the best and most enjoyable battle royale experiences I’ve ever played.

Generally a solid game, but the devs are holding it back big time, they should:

1. Focus less on this AI driven company garbage with AI team mates and focus more on improving the game for your actual human players. I don't mind having bots around to help newer players, but these bots shouldn't be the sole focus and AI team mates shouldn't even take up development resources.
2. Improve Anti Cheat
3. Make better balancing changes. You're nerfing DMRs yet MP5 is still allowed to laser beam you from mid range. I dont even need to mention Panzerfaust. C4 was also nerfed to complete uselessness.
4. Optimize the game more, FPS are pretty low and crashes are pretty annoying, especially in ranked
5. Give a better way of obtaining skins. A lot of skins aren't even obtainable anymore and if they are, you have to pray to get them out of a case with a 0.2% chance or lower. Also why do you have to unlock the same gun skin twice in Hideout to be able to craft it? Also the daily rewards are terrible, you get a handful of credits for surviving 240 minutes
6. Remove Rondo. There are periods where they spam us with Rondo for weeks but no region (not even Asia) likes it. 
7. Stop trying to make Esports TPP, it makes it boring to watch. Noone wants to watch pro players camp behind a wall until they see a free kill
8. Just generally listen to the community more and stop making weird changes. This whole AI driven company thing isn't something to proudly announce. Players want high quality, hand crafted content and not AI generated slop. Meanwhile Krafton can't even give us proper personalized offers in "Your Shop", which is something that AI could've actually been used for.
